documents.title: LHC Operations Overview and Operational Modes
documents.content:
The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) is a circular particle accelerator located at CERN, Geneva, operating at a circumference of 26.7 km. It accelerates proton beams or heavy-ion beams to near-light speeds and brings them into collision at four interaction points: ATLAS, CMS, ALICE, and LHCb. The machine operates at a nominal center-of-mass energy of 13.6 TeV for proton–proton collisions during Run 3. The superconducting dipole magnets bend the beams around the ring, operating at a field of 8.33 T and cooled to 1.9 K using superfluid helium.

An LHC operational cycle—referred to as a 'fill'—consists of several well-defined phases: injection, energy ramp, betatron squeeze, adjust, stable beams, and dump. During injection, proton beams are received from the SPS (Super Proton Synchrotron) at 450 GeV and captured in the LHC RF buckets. The ramp phase then accelerates both beams from 450 GeV to the flat-top energy over approximately 20 minutes using a ramping rate carefully matched to the rate of change of the dipole magnet current.

The squeeze phase reduces the transverse beam size at the interaction points (IP) by decreasing the beta-function (β*) from several meters down to as low as 30–40 cm, concentrating the beams for maximum luminosity. The adjust phase fine-tunes the crossing angles and orbit corrections at each IP. Once stable beams are declared by the experiments, physics data collection begins. A typical fill duration is 10–15 hours, after which beams are extracted to the beam dump blocks at point 6 for safe termination.

Key performance indicators monitored during operations include: instantaneous luminosity (target: 2×10³⁴ cm⁻²s⁻¹), beam intensity (up to 2.5×10¹¹ protons per bunch, 2748 bunches per beam), beam emittance, energy spread, and abort gap population. The LHC Operations team in the CERN Control Centre (CCC) monitors all systems 24/7 and coordinates with the experiments for optimal physics delivery.

documents.title: Beam Injection Procedures and SPS-to-LHC Transfer
documents.content:
Beam injection into the LHC is performed via two transfer lines: TI2 (connecting SPS to LHC Point 2 for Beam 1) and TI8 (connecting SPS to LHC Point 8 for Beam 2). Each injection line is approximately 2.5 km long and contains dipole and quadrupole magnets, beam position monitors (BPMs), and beam loss monitors (BLMs) to ensure safe and accurate beam transfer. Prior to injection, the transfer line is threaded with low-intensity pilot bunches to verify optical functions and beam trajectory.

The injection process is triggered by the LHC Timing System and coordinated with the SPS extraction. The SPS extracts batches of 288 bunches at a time into the TI lines using a fast-pulsed kicker magnet (MKE). On the LHC side, injection kicker magnets (MKI) deflect the incoming beam onto the LHC closed orbit. The injection septum magnets (MSI) provide the initial angular kick. Precise synchronization between SPS and LHC RF systems is essential—the bunch phase must match within ±1 ns to avoid emittance blow-up upon capture.

Injection quality is assessed by measuring the injected beam emittance using the synchrotron light monitor (BSRT) and the wire scanners (BWS). Orbit matching between the end of TI2/TI8 and the LHC is verified using trajectory measurements at multiple BPM stations. If beam losses exceed threshold values defined in the beam interlock controller (BIC), injection is automatically aborted and a post-mortem analysis is initiated to identify the cause.

A typical injection sequence fills the LHC with up to 2748 bunches per beam, injected in multiple SPS batches over 20–30 minutes. Between batches, beam quality is verified and the injection parameters may be adjusted. The injection process is fully automated under the LHC sequencer but can be paused or aborted by the operator at any time from the operational panel in the CCC.

documents.title: Magnet Quench Detection, Protection, and Recovery Procedures
documents.content:
A magnet quench is the sudden loss of superconductivity in a superconducting magnet, causing the coil resistance to rise from zero to a finite value. This results in rapid ohmic heating that can damage the coil if not promptly managed. In the LHC, all 1232 main dipole magnets and 392 main quadrupole magnets are superconducting, operating at 1.9 K. The Quench Protection System (QPS) is therefore a critical safety subsystem that monitors every magnet circuit continuously.

The QPS employs inductive voltage tap sensors across each magnet coil to detect the onset of a resistive zone. When a voltage imbalance above threshold (~100 mV for a sustained duration) is detected, a quench is declared. The system immediately fires the quench heaters—resistive strips embedded between the coil turns—to spread the resistive zone uniformly throughout the coil, preventing localized overheating. Simultaneously, the energy stored in the magnet circuit (up to 11 GJ for a main dipole circuit at 11.85 kA) is extracted via dump resistors through fast-acting energy extraction switches. The beam is also automatically dumped within microseconds via the beam dump system.

Post-quench recovery involves several steps: (1) diagnosis of the quench location and cause using QPS logging and thermal analysis; (2) cool-down verification — the affected sector must be confirmed stable at operating temperature (1.9 K) before re-powering; (3) magnet powering tests to verify the circuit integrity; (4) re-training if the quench was due to a first-time powering at a new peak current. Training quenches are expected for new magnets or after thermal cycles, as the coil mechanically settles at cryogenic temperature.

Typical quench recovery time ranges from 3 to 24 hours depending on severity. A so-called 'UFO' (Unidentified Falling Object) quench, caused by macroscopic dust particles falling into the beam halo and generating a local beam loss spike, can often be recovered in under 2 hours. A training quench or one accompanied by a sector warm-up may require days. All quench events are logged in the CERN logging service and trigger an automatic post-mortem data capture for offline analysis.

documents.title: Cryogenics System Operations and Temperature Management
documents.content:
The LHC cryogenic system is one of the largest in the world, maintaining approximately 37,000 tonnes of cold mass at 1.9 K—colder than outer space (2.7 K). The system uses superfluid helium (He-II) at 1.9 K and sub-atmospheric pressure (~1.3 kPa) to cool the superconducting magnets below their critical temperature. The LHC is divided into eight independent cryogenic sectors (Sectors 12, 23, 34, 45, 56, 67, 78, and 81), each served by a refrigeration plant at the corresponding even-numbered LHC point.

Each cryogenic sector has three main thermal levels: 1.9 K (superfluid helium bath for magnets), 4.5 K (cold compression stations and heat shields), and 50–75 K (thermal screens reducing heat load from 300 K environment). The refrigeration capacity per sector is approximately 18 kW at 4.5 K equivalent. The total installed refrigeration power for the entire LHC is about 144 kW at 4.5 K. The cryogenic control system (CRYOCON) manages thousands of temperature, pressure, and flow sensors, adjusting valve positions and compressor speeds via PID controllers.

Cooldown of a sector from 300 K to 1.9 K is a multi-day process controlled by the thermal gradient limit of ±50 K/m along the cryostat. First, gaseous helium at ~80 K is circulated through the magnets (using liquid nitrogen pre-cooling in the heat exchangers) to bring the mass to ~80 K over several days. Liquid helium at 4.5 K is then filled and the final step to 1.9 K is achieved by pumping on the superfluid helium with cold compressors, reducing the vapor pressure below the lambda point (2.17 K at 5 kPa). The entire LHC cooldown from room temperature typically takes 4–6 weeks.

Key alarm thresholds monitored by the cryo operator include: sector pressure exceeding 2.0 kPa (beam abort trigger), temperature excursion above 2.2 K (quench risk), cold compressor surge detection, and helium inventory depletion rate. Regular pressure relief tests, valve actuator checks, and level sensor calibrations are performed during technical stops. Unplanned warmups due to loss of insulating vacuum or helium leaks are among the most operationally disruptive events, potentially leading to weeks of downtime.

documents.title: RF Cavity Tuning and Beam Acceleration Procedures
documents.content:
The LHC RF system provides the longitudinal focusing and acceleration voltage for both beams. Each beam is served by four superconducting RF cavities operating at 400.789 MHz (harmonic number h = 35,640), grouped into two cryomodules per beam located in LHC Point 4. Each cavity delivers up to 2 MV peak voltage, providing a total of 8 MV per beam for acceleration and for counteracting synchrotron radiation energy loss (approximately 6.7 keV per turn at 6.5 TeV). The cavities are Nb/Cu type (niobium thin film on copper), cooled to 4.5 K.

Cavity tuning is performed by a mechanical piezoelectric tuner that adjusts the cavity resonant frequency to match the RF generator frequency, compensating for mechanical deformations and Lorentz detuning from the electromagnetic field. The Low-Level RF (LLRF) system provides fast digital feedback and feed-forward loops to maintain phase and amplitude stability at the 10⁻⁴ relative level. Frequency matching between the two beams and between LHC and SPS is essential during injection to achieve correct bunch capture and avoid emittance blow-up.

During the ramp, the RF frequency is adjusted synchronously with the dipole magnet ramp (controlled via the function generator) to maintain the beam on the central orbit as the momentum increases. The RF voltage is typically held constant during the ramp and then increased (to 12 MV per beam at flat top) during the squeeze phase to provide stronger longitudinal stability against collective instabilities. Additional Landau cavity systems at 800 MHz are used in some operating scenarios to improve beam stability by introducing controlled energy spread.

Diagnostic checks for RF system health include: reflected power ratio (coupling coefficient), cavity unloaded Q factor monitoring, multipacting detection via phase noise, input coupler temperature (indicative of surface resistance), and cryogenic heat load. A cavity that cannot maintain its voltage set-point due to a thermal run-away (quench) or a coupler failure is interlocked off and the remaining cavities must compensate, potentially reducing the maximum available voltage.

documents.title: Beam Dump System: Nominal and Emergency Extraction
documents.content:
The LHC Beam Dump System (BDS) is responsible for safely extracting both beams from the LHC and depositing their energy into two graphite/carbon-composite dump blocks located at Point 6 of the ring. Given the enormous stored energy of up to 362 MJ per beam at 6.5 TeV with nominal intensity, the dump system represents one of the most critical machine protection elements. The extraction is performed by a system of fast-pulsed kicker magnets (MKD — 15 modules per beam) combined with horizontal and vertical dilution kickers (MKBH/MKBV) that spread the extracted beam over the dump block face to avoid spot heating.

The dump trigger can originate from many sources: the Beam Interlock Controller (BIC), the operator via a software button, the Energy Interlock (EI) system, or automatically from the machine protection system (MPS). Once triggered, the MKD magnets must rise to full field within one turn (89 µs) to cleanly extract all bunches in a single extraction turn, avoiding partial extraction which could cause catastrophic beam losses in the ring. The abort gap—a 3 µs empty region in the beam—is maintained during physics to allow the kicker field to rise without intercepting bunches.

Dump quality is monitored by beam loss monitors and beam current transformers downstream of the extraction septa. A 'poor dump' (one where beam loss occurred in the ring during extraction, e.g., due to an asynchronous dump) triggers an automatic post-mortem analysis and must be investigated before re-injection. Asynchronous beam dumps are the most dangerous failure mode: if a dump fires at the wrong phase relative to the abort gap, beam can strike the extraction septum (TCDQ) and surrounding aperture, potentially causing severe material damage and radiation activation.

Regular dump tests are performed without beam and with low-intensity pilot beams to verify the kicker voltage waveforms, synchronization, and dump block monitoring instrumentation. The dump system also participates in the BIC validation cycle at the start of each fill, where a deliberate interlock is triggered to verify the complete protection chain from trigger to beam extraction.

documents.title: Collimation System: Hierarchy, Settings, and Loss Maps
documents.content:
The LHC collimation system is a multi-stage hierarchy of absorbers designed to intercept beam halo particles and protect sensitive equipment from the highly energetic beam. The system comprises over 100 collimator devices distributed around the ring, organized into a three-stage hierarchy: primary (TCP), secondary (TCSG), and tertiary (TCT) collimators, plus additional shower absorbers (TCLA) and Roman pot detectors (TCSP). The primary cleaning insertions are at IR7 (betatron cleaning) and IR3 (momentum cleaning).

The collimator jaw gaps are set in normalized beam sigma units (σ), with the hierarchy strictly maintained: primary jaws must be closest to the beam, secondaries slightly wider, and tertiaries wider still. Typical settings at flat top for proton-proton operations are: TCP at 5σ, TCSG at 6.5σ, TCT at 10σ. The correct gap hierarchy is enforced by the Collimator Database and verified automatically before transitions to higher intensity or lower β*. Any violation of the hierarchy triggers an interlock preventing beam operation.

Beam loss maps are generated periodically by intentionally driving beam losses using the transverse damper or by local orbit bumps, and measuring the resulting loss pattern on the BLM network. A clean loss map shows the vast majority of losses concentrated at the primary and secondary collimators in IR3 and IR7, with very low losses (< 1 BLM unit) at the superconducting magnets. A high loss rate at any cold element (superconducting magnet BLM) during physics may indicate a collimator hierarchy violation, an aperture restriction, or a UFO event.

During technical stops, collimator alignment is performed by scanning each jaw independently across the beam while monitoring beam losses, allowing the center of the beam to be precisely determined (to < 10 µm accuracy). Jaws are made of carbon-fiber composite (CFC) for the primary and secondary collimators, and tungsten for the tertiary collimators. The jaw surface flatness and impedance matching (longitudinal and transverse beam coupling impedance) are important design parameters affecting beam stability at high intensity.

documents.title: Vacuum Systems: Beam Pipe Pressure Management and Bake-out
documents.content:
The LHC beam vacuum system maintains ultra-high vacuum (UHV) in the beam pipes, typically at pressures below 10⁻¹⁰ mbar at cryogenic temperature, to minimize beam–gas interactions that cause beam loss, emittance growth, and beam lifetime degradation. The 27 km of beam pipe spans three distinct vacuum zones: the cold bore at 1.9 K (inside the cryostat), the beam screen at ~20 K (which intercepts photon and ion desorption before it reaches the cold bore), and the room-temperature sections at injection points and service modules.

The primary pumping mechanism at cryogenic temperature is cryosorption: gas molecules freeze onto the cold surfaces with extremely low vapor pressure. The beam screen acts as a first interception surface, pumped by NEG (Non-Evaporable Getter) strips. At room temperature in the warm sections, a combination of ion pumps, turbo-molecular pumps, and sputter-ion pumps maintains the required vacuum. Residual gas analyzers (RGAs) at strategic locations identify outgassing species, typically H₂, CO, CO₂, and CH₄.

Before installation, all beam pipe sections are cleaned (chemical etching, rinsing, drying) and baked out at 250–300°C for 24 hours under continuous pumping to drive off adsorbed water and hydrocarbons. In situ bake-out of warm sections is performed after any atmospheric venting, using resistive heater cables wrapped around the beam pipe. The bake-out process typically reduces the outgassing rate by 2–3 orders of magnitude and is essential for reaching UHV conditions within a reasonable pump-down time.

Beam-induced effects significantly impact vacuum pressure during operation. Synchrotron radiation from the circulating beam causes photon-stimulated desorption (PSD) of gas molecules from the beam screen surface, temporarily raising pressure. Ion-induced desorption occurs when residual gas ions are accelerated toward the beam pipe wall by the beam's space-charge potential, releasing surface gas. Electron-cloud formation in positively-charged bunched beams drives electron-stimulated desorption (ESD). Conditioning the beam screen surface with accumulated photon, ion, and electron dose progressively reduces these effects over the course of the run.

documents.title: Beam Diagnostics Instrumentation and Measurement Techniques
documents.content:
The LHC beam instrumentation system provides real-time information on all key beam parameters required for machine commissioning, routine operations, and protection. The system comprises over 500 individual instruments distributed around the ring, interfaced to the CERN accelerator controls framework (CMW/FESA). The primary diagnostic systems include: Beam Position Monitors (BPMs), Beam Current Transformers (BCTs), Beam Loss Monitors (BLMs), Wire Scanners (BWS), Synchrotron Light Monitors (BSRT), and Schottky noise monitors.

Beam Position Monitors (BPMs) measure the transverse position of the beam using button or stripline electrodes at over 1000 locations around the ring. Position data is used for closed-orbit correction, trajectory steering during injection, and optical model calibration. The orbit is corrected using over 1000 horizontal and vertical orbit corrector dipoles, with the MICADO algorithm or singular-value decomposition (SVD) technique applied to the BPM readings. Orbit reproducibility at the level of tens of micrometers is required at the interaction points.

Beam emittance is the key measure of beam quality. The BSRT (Beam Synchrotron Radiation Telescope) measures the transverse beam profile non-destructively using synchrotron light emitted by the beam in dipole magnets. Wire scanners provide destructive but highly accurate profile measurements during commissioning. The normalized transverse emittance (εₙ) is targeted at ≤2.5 µm-rad at injection and must be preserved through acceleration. Emittance growth during the fill is monitored and attributed to intra-beam scattering (IBS), noise from the RF system, or resonance driving terms from magnet errors.

Tune (fractional part of the betatron oscillation frequency, Qx and Qy) and chromaticity (dQ/dp) are fundamental parameters affecting beam stability. The AC dipole and the BBQ (Base-Band Tune) system measure the tune continuously by exciting coherent oscillations and analyzing the spectral content of the BPM signals. Chromaticity is measured by changing the RF frequency (and hence momentum) and observing the tune shift. Both parameters must be kept within narrow windows to avoid resonance conditions and collective instabilities.

documents.title: Machine Protection System and Interlock Architecture
documents.content:
The LHC Machine Protection System (MPS) is a hierarchical, redundant safety architecture designed to protect the machine from damage by the high-energy beam. Given that a single LHC beam stores up to 362 MJ of energy—enough to melt 500 kg of copper—the MPS must act within microseconds to extract the beam before damage occurs. The system is certified to achieve a beam dump response time of less than one revolution period (89 µs) from trigger to full field of the dump kicker.

The central element of the MPS is the Beam Interlock Controller (BIC), a dedicated hardware system (VME-based with FPGA logic) that receives input signals from all machine protection-relevant systems: power converters, cryogenics, vacuum, RF, collimation, and the experiments' own safety systems. Each subsystem sends a 'beam permit' signal to the BIC; if any single system withdraws its permit, the BIC issues a beam dump command to the beam dump system within a fixed latency of < 1 ms (software interlocks) or < 50 µs (hardware interlocks). The BIC operates in a fail-safe mode: loss of signal is interpreted as a dump request.

The Software Interlock System (SIS) provides a higher-level, configurable layer of interlocks for conditions that require more complex logic, such as checking collimator gap hierarchy or verifying magnet current ratios. SIS interlocks feed into the BIC via a dedicated interface. The Energy Interlock (EI) system monitors beam energy consistency between the RF frequency, the dipole magnet current, and the specified energy set-point, dumping the beam if an inconsistency exceeds tolerance—preventing a 'wrong energy' beam from being stored at the wrong optics configuration.

Post-mortem analysis is a cornerstone of the MPS. Every unintended beam loss or dump triggers an automatic capture of up to 100 MB of diagnostic data from all LHC subsystems, timestamped to 1 ms precision. The post-mortem data is archived and analyzed by the operator and on-call expert to determine the root cause. Common root causes include: power converter trips, UFO events, RF trips, interlock chain validation failures, and software errors. Lessons learned feed back into updated interlock thresholds and system firmware.

documents.title: Power Converter Systems and Magnet Circuit Management
documents.content:
The LHC main power converter systems provide the precisely regulated currents to the superconducting magnet circuits. The main dipole circuits in each of the eight sectors are powered by a single high-current power converter supplying up to 11,850 A at 190 V DC. These converters are connected in series to all 154 main dipole magnets in a sector, meaning that all dipoles in a sector always receive exactly the same current—essential for maintaining the closed orbit. The current regulation precision required is better than 1 ppm (parts per million) for stable beam operation.

The main quadrupole circuits are divided into independent circuits for the focusing (QF) and defocusing (QD) families, each powered by separate converters. The trim quadrupole circuits provide additional fine adjustment of the optics functions. Corrector magnet circuits (orbit, tune, chromaticity, coupling, higher-order multipoles) are powered by smaller, individually controlled converters. In total, the LHC has over 10,000 power converter channels, all interfaced to the CERN controls system via the Field Bus infrastructure.

Power converter trips—sudden loss of current regulation due to internal faults—are among the most frequent causes of unintended beam dumps. Common trip causes include: DC-link overvoltage (from back-EMF during magnet transitions), IGBT gate drive failures, water cooling alarms, regulation loop instabilities, and Earth fault detection. Each converter is equipped with arc detection electronics that sense a sudden voltage rise across the converter output (indicating a parallel arc discharge in the superconducting magnet bus bars) and immediately trigger the QPS and beam dump system.

Current ramping must follow pre-programmed functions (piecewise linear or polynomial) to maintain magnetic flux continuity and avoid inducing eddy currents in the beam screen. The ramp rate for the main dipole circuits is limited to approximately 10 A/s (equivalent to 0.11 GeV/s in beam energy) to respect the thermal load on the cryogenic system. Snap-back at injection flat bottom (the spontaneous change in sextupole field component as remnant magnetization equilibrates after the ramp) is a well-known effect corrected by pre-programmed feed-forward current functions in the chromaticity sextupoles.

documents.title: Water Cooling Systems for Accelerator Components
documents.content:
The LHC cooling infrastructure manages heat dissipation from all non-cryogenic accelerator components, including power converters, RF systems, normal-conducting magnets in the injection regions, experimental detector systems, and auxiliary equipment in the underground caverns and surface buildings. The cooling system uses demineralized water (DW) in closed primary circuits and raw water (from the Lake of Geneva water table) for final heat rejection through cooling towers, complying with strict CERN environmental regulations on thermal discharge.

The Primary Cooling Water (PCW) circuits are maintained at well-defined temperature set-points (typically 25–35°C supply) and pressure levels, with conductivity monitored and controlled below 5 µS/cm to prevent electrochemical corrosion. Flow meters, differential pressure sensors, and temperature probes at hundreds of locations provide real-time monitoring of cooling system health. Abnormal flow or temperature conditions trigger alarms in the control room and, if critical components (such as RF couplers or kicker magnets) are affected, automatic power reduction or beam dump interlocks.

The warm LHC magnets (normal-conducting elements such as the injection and dump kicker magnets) are cooled by dedicated circuits separate from the main power converter circuits. The RF coupler cooling circuits are particularly sensitive, as a loss of cooling to an RF coupler within seconds can lead to thermal damage at the megawatt power level of the coupler. Water temperature rise across an RF coupler is continuously calculated from inlet/outlet temperature sensors and compared against the expected value from the cavity power measurement.

Annual maintenance of the cooling system includes: water treatment chemical dosing checks, heat exchanger inspection and de-scaling, pump seal replacement, and calibration of flow and conductivity instrumentation. During extended technical stops, the primary circuits may be drained and flushed to remove corrosion products and biofilm. The cooling towers are inspected for Legionella and maintained per CERN Health, Safety, and Environmental (HSE) unit guidelines.

documents.title: Personnel Access Control and Radiation Safety
documents.content:
Access to the LHC tunnel and the adjacent experimental caverns is controlled by a multi-layer Personnel Protection System (PPS) that ensures no person is present in a radiation zone when the beam is operational or when high-voltage equipment is energized. The LHC ring is divided into 8 sectors, each with a dedicated access control point equipped with biometric readers, key banks, and safety light barriers. The PPS operates independently of the machine control system and is SIL-2 certified (Safety Integrity Level).

The access procedure follows a strict protocol: (1) Request access via the CERN access control software (IMPACT), specifying the target zone, purpose, and number of personnel; (2) The CCC operator checks that the beam is off and that the requested zone is safe (radiation survey completed, no stored energy); (3) A physical sweep of the zone is performed with a portable radiation monitor before key release; (4) Personnel retrieve their personal safety key from the key bank, which physically prevents the tunnel segment from being re-enabled while the key is not returned; (5) Portable dosimeters (active TLD badges) are worn throughout the visit; (6) At exit, keys are returned, and the access system re-enables the zone only after all keys are returned and a headcount confirms all personnel are out.

Radiation zones in and around the LHC are classified according to CERN's radiation protection rules, based on the expected dose rate: Non-Designated (< 1 µSv/h), Simple Supervised (< 0.5 mSv/week), Limited Stay (< 2 mSv/h average), High Radiation (< 100 mSv/h), Very High Radiation (≥ 100 mSv/h), and Prohibited (restricted to remote operation only). The highest dose rates occur around the beam dump blocks, the collimators, and the experimental interaction points during technical stops after extended high-luminosity running. ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) principles govern all work planning near radiation sources.

Accident scenarios in the access system are handled by the Emergency Response Team (ERT) and the CERN Fire and Rescue Service. Emergency escape routes are posted throughout the tunnel every 300 m, and emergency evacuation exercises are conducted annually. Communication within the tunnel relies on both fixed interphone stations and personal radio units, both connected to the CCC and the CERN telephone exchange.

documents.title: Luminosity Optimization Techniques and Leveling Strategies
documents.content:
Luminosity (L) at the LHC interaction points is the key physics productivity metric, directly proportional to the collision rate of interest: R = L × σ, where σ is the cross-section of the physics process. For proton–proton physics at 13.6 TeV, the peak luminosity target is 2×10³⁴ cm⁻²s⁻¹ for the ATLAS (IP1) and CMS (IP5) experiments, and lower controlled values for ALICE (IP2) and LHCb (IP8). Luminosity is determined by the beam intensity, transverse emittance, and the β* value at the IP.

Peak luminosity occurs at the start of a fill when beam intensity is highest. To protect the experiments from excessively high instantaneous luminosity (which increases pile-up—the average number of simultaneous p-p interactions per bunch crossing, targeted at 50–60 for Run 3), luminosity leveling is applied. Leveling strategies include: (1) β* leveling: the β* is kept at a value larger than the minimum achievable and progressively squeezed as the beam intensity decays; (2) crossing-angle leveling: the full crossing angle is introduced and progressively reduced; (3) transverse offset leveling: a deliberate transverse offset between beams is applied at the IP and reduced with time. Each strategy has different implications for beam stability and machine protection.

Optical matching at the IP is critical for luminosity performance. Residual beta beating (deviation from design optics) must be below 5% peak-to-peak, and the coupling (energy transfer between horizontal and vertical planes) must be minimized (|C⁻| < 10⁻³). Optical corrections are performed at the start of each fill using response matrix measurements (orbit response matrix, tune and coupling response) and feed-forward correction using dedicated local correction knobs. The k-modulation technique measures β* in situ at the IP by varying individual quadrupole currents and observing the tune shift.

Integrated luminosity (∫L dt) delivered per fill and per run period is tracked by both CERN and the experiments. Machine efficiency—the fraction of calendar time during which beams are in stable collisions—is the key operational KPI, typically targeted above 35% for a full year. Losses of machine time are tracked using the CERN Operational Efficiency reporting tool, categorizing downtime by subsystem (cryo, RF, magnets, vacuum, beam dump, access, etc.) to guide prioritization of maintenance and improvement activities.

documents.title: Emergency Shutdown Protocols and Incident Response
documents.content:
Emergency shutdown procedures at the LHC are designed to bring the machine to a safe state within the minimum possible time, protecting both personnel and equipment. The primary automated response for beam-related emergencies is beam extraction to the dump blocks via the LBDS, which occurs within one revolution (89 µs) upon receipt of a validated dump request. For magnet and cryogenic emergencies, the Quench Protection System discharges the magnet energy into the dump resistors. These two systems operate independently and in parallel without requiring CCC operator intervention.

The CCC operator role during an emergency is primarily to monitor the automated response, communicate with on-call experts and the CERN emergency services, and initiate further actions such as: powering down magnet circuits manually, isolating vacuum sectors, contacting the Radiation Protection (RP) group, or initiating an access for emergency repair. Emergency contacts and escalation trees are maintained in the CERN Emergency Operations document (accessible on the CERN operations portal) and reviewed at every shift changeover briefing.

Physical emergency scenarios specific to the LHC tunnel and caverns include: fire (tunnel ventilation reverses automatically to confine smoke in the incident sector), Oxygen Deficiency Hazard (ODH) from helium or nitrogen gas leaks (fixed gas detectors throughout the tunnel alarm and interlock tunnel entry), and electrical faults in HV equipment. Magnet quench events involving large helium releases trigger ODH alarms and mandatory evacuation of the affected sector. Sector isolation valves in the helium distribution system automatically close to limit the gas release volume.

The Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) methodology is applied to all new LHC systems and to periodic reviews of existing systems to systematically identify potential failure modes, their causes, and their effects on the machine and safety. FMEA findings feed into the interlock design, the maintenance schedule, and the operational procedures. Annual emergency drills involving the LHC operators, the CERN Fire and Rescue Service, and the ERT team test the coordination and response time of the emergency system. Lessons learned from incidents—whether at CERN or at peer laboratories—are reviewed in the LHC Operations Committee and incorporated into updated procedures.
